items.q: Does an interpreter create a machine-code file you can run later?
items.correct: No - an interpreter translates and executes the program line by line each run and saves no executable; producing a stored executable is what compilers do.
items.misconception: Yes - interpreters and compilers both output an executable file.
items.random_wrong: Yes - interpreters save their translation as a text document.
items.why: Both translators make code run, hiding the artefact difference. Remember: compiled programs can be shipped without the source; interpreted programs cannot.

items.q: Are assembly language and machine code the same thing?
items.correct: No - assembly uses human-readable mnemonics like ADD and LDA that map one-to-one onto machine code's binary instructions; an assembler converts between them.
items.misconception: Yes - assembly is just machine code printed on paper.
items.random_wrong: Yes - assembly is a high-level language like Python.
items.why: The tight one-to-one mapping makes them feel identical. The mnemonic layer exists purely for humans, and the assembler is the translator that bridges the gap.

items.q: Is an IDE a programming language?
items.correct: No - an IDE is a software tool for writing, running and debugging programs; the language is what you write, the IDE is where you write it.
items.misconception: Yes - Python and an IDE are the same kind of thing.
items.random_wrong: Yes - installing an IDE installs a new language automatically.
items.why: Beginners meet language and editor at the same moment, fusing them. One language can be used in many IDEs, and one IDE can support many languages.

items.correct: Binary instructions executed directly by the CPU
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Binary instructions executed directly by the CPU. “An English-like scripting language” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which translator produces a standalone executable file?
items.why: Correct answer: A compiler. “An interpreter” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: One advantage of high-level languages over low-level languages is that they are:
items.correct: Closer to natural language, so quicker to write and easier to debug
items.misconception: Executed directly by the CPU with no translation
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Closer to natural language, so quicker to write and easier to debug. “Executed directly by the CPU with no translation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which IDE feature lets you pause a running program at a chosen line to inspect variable values?
items.why: It's A breakpoint in the debugger. “The spell checker” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
